title: Before Angkor: Funan, Chenla, and Indianization
bullets: The Tonlé Sap river reverses and flows backward every monsoon, quadrupling the lake from about 1,000 to 6,000 square miles and leaving behind fresh silt and one of the richest inland fisheries on Earth.
That reversing lake, not the coast, is why a great civilization grew up inland here: it handed whoever could organize the people around it free protein and rebuilt soil, year after year.

Funan, the first named state, ran the port of Oc Eo, where excavators found second-century Roman coins, Han Chinese mirrors, and Indian gold — a Mediterranean-to-China trade route four centuries before Angkor.

Indianization brought Sanskrit, Shiva and Vishnu, Buddhism, Indian scripts and statecraft — and India never sent a soldier; local elites pulled the prestige culture toward themselves because it was useful.
The Funan founding legend has the Brahmin Kaundinya marry the local naga princess Soma: the foreign holy man weds the indigenous water-goddess, Indianization told as a marriage.
The Khmer spoke Austroasiatic, a deep linguistic root unrelated to Chinese, to India, and crucially to the Austronesian languages of the coast — a gap that nearly destroys the empire in Chapter 6.
Inscription K.557, dated 611–612 CE, is the oldest securely dated text in Khmer and one of the oldest in any Southeast Asian vernacular; the poetry was in Sanskrit, the tax accounting in Khmer.

Chenla's brick temple-city of Sambor Prei Kuk, built around 616, is the largest pre-Angkorian monument and the prototype: Angkor inherited a temple tradition already two centuries deep and rebuilt it in sandstone.

title: Jayavarman II and the God-King
bullets: In 802 on Phnom Kulen, a Brahmin consecrated Jayavarman II as a universal monarch and instituted the devaraja, the god-king cult, explicitly to make Cambodia no longer dependent on Java.

Almost everything about the founding comes from the Sdok Kok Thom inscription, carved 250 years later in 1052 by a family of priests glorifying their own hereditary office — a charter myth, not a transcript.
Even what the devaraja was is contested: a literal cult of the king's divine essence, or just royal sponsorship of a state god? The texts genuinely do not settle what kind of thing a Khmer king was.

Divine kingship was not unique to Cambodia — Egypt's pharaohs were living gods, the Maya had holy lords — but the Khmer version's distinctive feature was the physical scale of building it justified for six centuries.
Jayavarman II ruled from Hariharalaya, named for Harihara, a single deity who is Shiva and Vishnu fused into one body — a very Khmer answer to which great Hindu god to back: both, in one statue.
Indravarman I, ruling from about 877, set the template every later king copied: build a baray reservoir, build an ancestor temple, build your own state temple-mountain.
His Bakong was the first major sandstone temple-mountain at Angkor; the formula water, ancestors, mountain is the thing the next five centuries of this story keep repeating, getting bigger.

The ninth-century record is so thin that Jayavarman III is remembered mainly as a legendary elephant-hunter — a fair warning about how much of the founding dynasty is reconstructed from a handful of stones.

title: The Hydraulic City: Yasodharapura and the Barays
bullets: In 889 Yasovarman I moved the capital onto the Angkor plain and founded Yasodharapura; Angkor just means the City, the Khmer pronunciation of the Sanskrit word for city, not a name.

Every big temple is a stepped pyramid because it is Mount Meru, the cosmic mountain at the center of the universe, built at buildable scale — towers as the gods' peak, walls as the world's mountains, moat as the cosmic ocean.
Yasovarman built the East Baray, an artificial lake about 4.5 by 1.1 miles you could not see across; later kings added the even larger West Baray, which still holds water today.
Khmer succession had no firm rule, so the throne was contested by war for six centuries; around 928 the usurper Jayavarman IV abandoned Angkor entirely for a new capital at Koh Ker.

Banteay Srei, consecrated in 967, breaks every rule: tiny, built by a Brahmin teacher named Yajnavaraha rather than a king, and carved from rose-pink sandstone so finely the French refused to believe its age.
André Malraux tried to steal several tons of Banteay Srei's carvings in 1923 and was arrested — an early scene in the long story of Angkor's looting that runs to Chapter 10.

Groslier's 1979 hydraulic-city thesis said the barays fed a vast irrigation network whose silting-up later caused the collapse; skeptics said the reservoirs were mainly cosmological and for urban water supply.
The current honest answer is both, and bigger than either side guessed — and the same disagreement, scaled up, is the still-unsettled argument about why Angkor ended.

title: The Eleventh Century: Khleang Kings, Baphuon, Suryavarman I
bullets: Suryavarman I won the throne after roughly eight years of civil war, then did the unglamorous work: he expanded the state west and tried to fix the thing that kept breaking it.
He pushed Khmer power into the Mon kingdom of Lavo, modern Lopburi in central Thailand — the exact western ground from which the Thai kingdoms would later rise and destroy Angkor.
With no reliable bureaucracy, Suryavarman I had an oath of allegiance sworn by some 4,000 officials and carved into the palace gateway — government by mass loyalty oath set in stone.
The contemporary Northern Song ran a vast professional civil service recruited by written examination; Angkor and Song were equally sophisticated but ran on completely different operating systems.
Udayadityavarman II built the huge Baphuon temple-mountain and the West Baray, and faced repeated revolts including the rebellion of the general Kamvau — grand temples above, rebellion below.

The eleventh-century pattern is stark: grand temple, giant reservoir, expensive western wars, and armed rebellion at home — the Khmer state was a machine that shook harder the more aggressively it ran.
Preah Vihear, strung along an 800-foot cliff on the Dangrek escarpment, is the most spectacularly sited building the Khmer ever made.
It is also still live ammunition: the World Court has had to rule twice, in 1962 and 2013, on the modern Cambodia–Thailand border combat over an eleventh-century Shiva temple.

title: Suryavarman II and Angkor Wat
bullets: Suryavarman II took the throne around 1113 by attacking and killing his great-uncle in battle, leaping onto the enemy war-elephant as Garuda kills a serpent — succession reframed as a god's eagle destroying a snake.

He sent embassies to Song China and launched repeated invasions of Dai Viet and Champa, sacking the Cham capital Vijaya in 1145 — aggression that brought the catastrophic Cham counterstroke of Chapter 6.
Angkor Wat is, by enclosed footprint, the largest religious monument ever built; any Gothic cathedral of the same decades would fit inside its outer enclosure several times over.
Almost every Khmer temple honors Shiva and faces east; Angkor Wat is dedicated to Vishnu and faces west, toward death — the leading reading is that it was Suryavarman II's own funerary temple.

The temple is a deliberate model of the cosmos: five central towers as the peaks of Mount Meru, galleries as the world's mountain ranges, the moat as the cosmic ocean, walked as a passage to the center of the universe.

Roughly half a mile of continuous bas-relief wraps the third level, including the Churning of the Sea of Milk: gods and demons using the serpent Vasuki to churn the ocean for the elixir of immortality.
One gallery shows Suryavarman II himself reviewing his army with his name carved beside him — one of the only securely identified contemporary portraits of any Khmer king.
He died around 1150, probably on a failed campaign, and the throne fell into exactly the contested weak-handed succession this book keeps warning about.

title: The Cham Catastrophe: the 1177 Sack and the Interregnum
bullets: After Suryavarman II the throne went through usurpers and weak kings, and the empire that had been launching invasions a generation earlier was now divided, distracted, and badly led.

The Cham spoke an Austronesian language — the seafaring island family of Malay and Polynesian — as far from Khmer as an Englishman from a Malay sailor; Champa was a maritime power where the Khmer were riverine.
In 1177 a Cham fleet under Jaya Indravarman IV came by water — up the Mekong, across the Tonlé Sap — and took Yasodharapura by surprise, killed the king, and burned the capital.
Champa then occupied the heartland of Angkor for roughly four years, from 1177 to about 1181 — the single greatest catastrophe in Khmer history before 1431.
This is the hinge of the whole book: there is no Angkor Wat on a flag, no Bayon, no hospitals, in the timeline where 1177 is permanent.
It was reversed by a royal prince already in his late fifties who emerged from obscurity to organize the resistance.
To beat a sea power that came by river, the Khmer had to win on the river: he won a decisive battle fought on the water of the Tonlé Sap itself, then was crowned around 1181.

He later carved the war in vivid, brutal detail onto the Bayon's reliefs — capsizing canoes, crocodiles eating the drowned — close to a documentary record of the catastrophe by the man who reversed it.

title: Jayavarman VII: the Bodhisattva King and the Bayon
bullets: Jayavarman VII was already roughly sixty when crowned around 1181 over a burned, recently occupied capital; in about forty years he built more of Angkor than every king before him combined.

He recast kingship in Mahayana Buddhist terms as a bodhisattva-king — a ruler whose sacred role was to relieve his people's suffering, not to be a god — which became a building permit for enormous public works.
He built Angkor Thom, a walled city 2.2 miles a side entered through gates topped with huge serene faces, and at its dead center the Bayon.
The Bayon bristles with around 200 giant calm faces; whether they are the bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara, the king himself, or deliberately both at once is genuinely unsettled among scholars.
Ta Prohm's foundation inscription functions as a census, recording on the order of 12,640 people resident at one temple plus tens of thousands more in attendant villages — a temple as a town.
Identical stone hospital edicts across the empire document the famous 102 hospitals, and his royal roads were lined with 121 rest-houses spaced a day's travel apart.
Between about 1203 and 1220 Khmer armies conquered and outright annexed Champa, the kingdom that had sacked Angkor in 1177 — the hinge swung all the way back.
The exhaustion thesis says his colossal building broke the empire, but it is a debated hypothesis; what is solid is that after he died around 1218 no king ever built on that scale again.

title: The Empire at Its Height: Society, Trade, and Zhou Daguan
bullets: A useful corrective: the best description of Angkor as a living, prosperous city comes from after the supposedly ruinous Jayavarman VII — the empire did not fall over when its greatest king died.
Jayavarman VIII presided over a furious Hindu reaction: thousands of Buddha images across the Bayon and the great temples were systematically smashed or recut into Shiva lingas, still visible today.
In 1296 the Yuan envoy Zhou Daguan reached Angkor — the same Mongol world, almost the same decade, that produced Marco Polo's account of China — and stayed about eleven months.
His short book, the Customs of Cambodia, is the only detailed eyewitness account of daily life in the entire Khmer Empire that exists; everything textured we can say comes substantially from it.

He describes a wealthy hierarchical city where, to his surprise, the traders were overwhelmingly women, alongside a large population of bonded laborers and slaves and a brutal trial-by-ordeal justice.
Greater Angkor's population estimates run from a few hundred thousand to nearly a million, depending entirely on the unresolved rice-cropping question of how many harvests a year the land produced.
Around 1295 the court turned to Theravada Buddhism, which needs no god-king, no Mount Meru in sandstone, no corvée army hauling stone — only monks and a modest village monastery.
The last great royal Sanskrit inscription dates to 1327; the religious justification for the whole Angkorian state simply evaporated from below, one village monastery at a time.

title: The Long Retreat and the 1431 Abandonment
bullets: The most important fact is what stopped: after Jayavarman VII no Khmer king ever built another great stone temple, and the silence in the stone after 1220 is the clearest evidence the civilization had changed.
Tai-speaking peoples founded their own kingdoms on the Khmer template — Sukhothai around 1238, then far more dangerously Ayutthaya in 1351 on the lower Chao Phraya.

Ayutthaya took Khmer statecraft, ritual, art, and vocabulary learned from Angkor and turned the whole apparatus against its teacher; the western frontier Suryavarman I opened produced Angkor's undertaker.
Siamese campaigns hit Angkor repeatedly across the late 1300s and early 1400s, and in 1431 an Ayutthayan army took the city and the court did not move back.
The textbook framing is wrong twice: 1431 was the loudest event in a two-century retreat already underway for reasons that had nothing to do with Ayutthaya.
The court relocated south toward Phnom Penh, near the river confluence and closer to the booming maritime trade a southern capital was better placed to exploit — a strategic move as much as a flight.
Angkor was never abandoned: the capital moved, but Angkor Wat itself was converted into a Theravada monastery and continuously maintained, with Khmer kings and Japanese pilgrims still visiting.
The lost-city-swallowed-by-jungle story most people think they know about Angkor is a myth, and a revealing one about who gets to count as having known a thing.

title: The Collapse Debate and Afterlife: the Jungle, Mouhot, LiDAR, Angkor as Symbol
bullets: Groslier's hydraulic-city thesis dominated for a generation: Angkor's aging waterworks silted up and the civilization collapsed under the failure of its own infrastructure.

In 2010 tree-ring evidence showed the 1300s and 1400s swung between severe multi-decade droughts and violent monsoons — close to a worst case for a civilization that was a centuries-long bet on managing water.

The current consensus is multi-causal and deliberately undramatic: climate shocks, the Theravada shift, the pull of maritime trade, and Ayutthayan war converging on a capital already hollowed of its purpose.
Angkor and the Classic Maya are the two great test cases in the global collapse debate, and both teach the same lesson: collapse is almost always too simple a word for a slow convergence.

Henri Mouhot is credited with discovering Angkor in 1860, but he discovered nothing — Angkor Wat was a functioning monk-tended temple locals guided him to; he publicized it, which is not the same thing.
From 1907 the École française d'Extrême-Orient ran a century of clearance and anastylosis — reassembling collapsed temples from their own fallen stones, including the Baphuon's million-piece puzzle.
The twentieth century did what no medieval enemy could: Khmer Rouge occupation, landmines, and statue looting did more damage in two decades than the previous five centuries of jungle.

LiDAR flown from 2007 revealed Angkor as a low-density urban sprawl of about 1,000 square kilometers — among the largest pre-industrial settlements ever found, and the only building on any national flag is now its spine of identity.
